California
Fair Political
Practices Cornmission
May 1L, 1990
Mary L. Leach
527 South L Street
Lompoc, CA 93436
Re: 5::'"li""T:: f5.l$i"a1 
Assistance
Dear Ms. Leach:
This tetter is in response to your request for confirmation
of the telephone advice provided to you on February 27 , L99o,
concerning lour duties and responsibilities under the conflict-of-
i;t;;;;a 
;t'ovisions of the pot-iticat Reforn Act (the rraglrrl '1
Because tie facts in your letter differ from those prowided during
our teLephone conversition, we are unable to confirm the advice as
provided .
Your request for advice does not refer to a specific
governmental decision but rather seeks guidance regarding
disqualification issues arj-sing from various decisions.
Theiefore, we treat your l-etter as a request for informal^as-
sistance pursuant to regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).2
OUESTION
Do the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act require you
to disqualify yourself from participating in decisions regarding
(1) th; rezoning of the Knoell property, (2) a building moratorium
on'l-and designaied R-2 and R-3, and (3) appl-ication of certain
ordinances?
CONCLUSION
The conffict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act require you to
disqualify from participating in decisions which will have a
fordseeable and rnaterial efftct on your financial interests which
is distinguishabLe from the effect upon the public generally'
t 
covernment Code sections 81OOo-91015. A11 statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
Cornmissi-on
regulations appear at 2 California code of Regulations . section
r.8ooo, et seq. Al1 references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California code of Regulations'
2 rnforrnal assistance does not
irnrnunity provided by an oPinion
tion 83114; Regul-ation 18329(c)
provide the requestor with the
or formal written advice' 
(Sec-
)
428 J Street, Suite 8OO . P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 o (916) 322-5660


FiIe No. I-9 0-181
Page 2
FACTS
You are a planning commissioner for the city of Lompoc. on
December 5, L9a9 , prior to your appointment to the planning com-
mission, the city council approved u!. Michael Knoell's app),ica-
tion to rezone his lot from R-2 to PD for purposes of dividing the
land into three lots to be sold independently. on or about
December ]-7, L989, the city suspended !tr. Knoell's permit on a
third buildi.ng proposed for the lot which is already developed
with two homes.
On January 2, L99O, opponents of the rezoning, including
yourself, gave testimony to the city council to the effect that
Lhe rezoning was in violation of existing ordinances. The city
council iniiiatea a moratorium on afl R-2 buildinq, referred the
rezoni,ng application back to the planning eomnissi.on for
reconsideration and referred certain ordinances to staff for
review and clarification.
Irnruediatety after your appointment to the planning commis-
sion, the city council amended the moratorium on buildj-nq to
include propeity zoned R-3. when the building moratorium was
scheduled for consideration by the planning conmission, the city
attorney advised you that you might have a disqualifying conflict
of inteiest. 
You then called the conmission for advice.
In the course of our telephone conversation you advised rne
that, prior to your apPointment to the planning comroission, you
had exgrressed your opposition to the proiect in the locaL
newspaper and before the city council. 
You were not compensated
tor thlse efforts. 
r inquired $rhether you had any business or
real property interests which night requi-re your disqualification
and y-ou infornea me that you did not. I then advised you.that if
you irad not received any paynents for pursuing your. opposition to
Lhe rezoning, and if you did not have any economic interests that
would requiie your disgualification, 
you need not abstain from
participiting 
in decisions regarding the rezoning at issue.
tfris aavice was based on i.nformation available to ne at the tine
of our telephone conversation.3
ANALYSIS
section 87100 prohibits any public official 
fron making,
participating in naking, or using his or her official 
position to
influence a governmental decision in which he or she knows or has
3 Yo.lr letter indicates that you have certain economic interests
in the city. 
our advice to you sras not based on and did not
consider those econonic interests because the infornation was not
available to us at the tiroe of our telephone conversation. we are
not addressing those specific interests at this tine but we are
providing you with a discussion of the conflict-of- interest provi-
sions of the Act to assist you in the future to determine whether
or not you have a conflict of interest.


File No, r-90-181
Page 3
reason to know he or she has a financial interest. 
an official
has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial ef-
fect, distingruishable frou its effect on the pubLic qenerallv, on
the official 
or a member of his irn'nediate faroily, or on:
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment f,rorth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.
(b) Any real property in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars (91,000) or more.
(c) Any source of income other than gifts and
other than loans by a connercial lending institu-
tion in the reqular course of business on terms
available to the public lrithout regard to official
status aggEegating two hundred fifty dollars ($250)
or more in vaLue provided to, received by or
pronised to the public official within 12 months
prior to the tine then the decision is rnade.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of Danagement.
***
For purposes of this section, indirect invest-
ment or interest Eeans any investment or interest
owned by the spouse or dependent child of a public
official, 
by an agent on behalf of a Public of-
ficial, 
or by a business entity or trust in which
the official, 
the official's 
agents, spouse, and
dependent children own directly, indirectly, or
beneficiaLly a 1O-Percent interest or greater.
Section 87103 (a) - (d) .
By virtue of your position as a planninq conmissioner you are a
public official. 
(Section a2o4a.) Accordingly, you must
disqualify yourself frorn participating in any decision which will,
have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on
your f inancj.al interests which is distingruishable from the effect
on the public generally.
Foreseeabi l itrr
The effects of a decision are reasonably foreseeable if there
is a substantial Likelihood that they will occur. To be foresee-
able, the effects of a decision must be nore than a urere possibil-
ity; however certainty is not required. 
(Dovrnev Cares v. Downev
Communitv Development Com. (1987) 196 CaI. App. 3d 983, 989-997;
Witt v. Morrow (L977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 817; In re Thorner (1975) 1
rPPC Ops. 198 (copy enclosed) . ) The Act seeks to prevent more


File No. I-9 0-181
Page 4
than actual conflicts of interest, it seeks to prevent even the
appearance of a possible conflict of interest. 
(witt v. Morrovr,
supra at 823. )
In order to determine lrhether the effect of a decision on
your financial j-nterests is foreseeable, you must anaLyze the pos-
sible effect of the decision on your financial interests. 
r.or
example, you must consider whether approval or disapproval of an
application to rezone a certain parcel will result in an increase
or decrease of factors such as changes in traffic patterns,
increased density, or greater noise Ieve1s which may increase or
decrease the value of your financial interests. 
Even erhen you
determine that the effect of a decision is foreseeable, you need
only disqualify frou participation when the effect of the decision
will be material, and such effect will be distinguishable fron the
effect upon the public aenerally.
Materia l itv
The commission has enacted reg"ulations which provide
guidelines for determining when the effect of a decision is nate-
rial. 
when the real property in which the public officiaL has an
interest is indirectly involved in a decision, the effect of the
decision is material if:
(a) (1) The real property in which the of-
ficial 
has an interest, or any Part of that real
property, is located within a 3oo foot radius of
the boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the
property which is the subject of the decision, un-
less the decision wilt have no financial effect
upon the official's 
real property interest.
***
(3) The real property in which the official
has an interest is located outside a radius of 300
feet and any part of the real property is J.ocated
within a radius which is the subject of the deci-
sion and the decision wj.I1 have a reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of:
(A) Ten thousand dollars ($10,000) or
nore on the fair narket value of the real
property in which the official 
has an
interest; ....
Regulation La7o2.3 (a) (1) and (3).
You have stated in your request for advice that you olrn a
horne which is located within 2,500 feet of the parceJ, which is the
object of the rezoning decision. ff your hone were Lrithin 3OO
feet of the parcel under consideration by the planning commission,
your disqualification would be required unless you could
demonstrate that the decision woul-d have no financial effect on
the value of your home. ConverseLy, because your home is located


File No. I-90-181
Page 5
outside a radius of 3oo feet from the project but within a 2,500
foot radius, then disqualif ication is not required unless the
decision is Iikely to increase or decrease the value of your irorne
by $10,00o or more.
You have also indicated in your request for confirmation of
telephone advj.ce that you own a small busj-ness in the city of
l,ompoc. when a busj.ness entity is indirectly involved in a deci-
sion, Regulation 18702.3 (copy enclosed) provides gnridelines,
based on the financial size of the business entity, for determin-
ing when the effect of a decision is material-. Because you have
indicated that your business is snaII, subdivision (S) of Regula-
tion 18702.3 probably describes the applicable standards which
would reguire disqualif ication if:
(L) The decision will result in an increase or
decrease in the gross revenues for a fiscal year of
910,000 or more; or
(2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additionaL expenses or
reducinq or elininating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of 52,500 or more; or
(3) The decision will
or decrease in the value of
of $10,000 or more.
result in an increase
assets or liabilities
Regulation L8702.2 (S), copy enclosed.
Clients of your business are considered sources of income to
you (Section 82030), and thus must be considered in analyzing your
duties under the Act. 
(see section 87103.) If you are the sole
owner of your business, any client who has paid or proroised $250
or more in gross revenues to your business during the past 12
months is consideied a source of income totaling $250 or more to
you. If the decisions in question $rould foreseeably and materi-
a1ly affect any of those clj.ents, you would be reguired to
disqualify yourself from participating j.n those decisj.ons.
The facts you have provided indicate that no clients of your
business would be directly affected by the decisions in question.
we do not have sufficient information to deterrnine whether the
indirect effects on any of your cLients wouLd be rnateriaL and thus
would require your disqualification frou any of the decisions in
question.
Public cenerallv
Even $/hen the reasonably foreseeabLe financial effect of a
decision is rnaterial, disgua lification 
is required only if the
effect of the decision on your financial interests is distinguish-
able from the effect on the public Arenerally. (Section 87103.)
For city planning commissioners, the public consists of aIl,
residents of the city. 
Thus, disqualification is required unless


File No. I-9o-r-81
Page 6
the decision will affect your financial interests in substantially
the same manner as it will affect all residents of the city, or a
significant segrment of the residents of the city. 
(Regulation
LA7O3, copy enclosed. )
we have insufficient information to deternine whether the
rezoningT of the Knoell property, the building moratorium on land
designated R-2 and R-3, and the review and clarification 
of
certain ordinances will affect your financial interests in a rnan-
ner that is distingnrishable from the effect on at1 other residents
of the city or a substantial nurober of those residents, In order
to make this deternination, you nust assess the consequences of
the decision, and then ascertain the effect of the decision on the
residents of Lonpoc. For example, rezoning that provides for
increased population density results in increased pollution, traf-
fic congestion, and dernand for services. In general, residents in
the inmediate vicinity of high density areas feel the impact of
the increased population to a greater degEee than those residents
of the city who reside farther away fron the project. 
Once you
have deternined the iropact of a decision on all or a substantial-
number of residents of the eity, you are in a position to
determine whether the public generally exception applies to your
facts .
Enclosed please find a copy of the Political Reform Act and a
I]amDhlet entitted californi-a's conflict of fnterest Law for Public
officials. 
Should you have further guestions regarding the
conf lict-of -interest provisions of the Act, do not hesitate to
contact ne at (915) 322-590]-.
s incerely,
Kathryn E. Donovan
General counsel
